_catalog.name: CargoTrack Sanctions Watch Catalog
sources: U.S. Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) — Sanctions Programs and Country Information
U.S. Bureau of Industry and Security (BIS) — Country Chart (EAR Supplement No. 1 to Part 738)
EU Consolidated List of Persons, Groups and Entities Subject to EU Financial Sanctions
UN Security Council Consolidated Sanctions List
UK OFSI (Office of Financial Sanctions Implementation) Consolidated List
Financial Action Task Force (FATF) — High-Risk Jurisdictions and Jurisdictions Under Increased Monitoring

mandatoryScreeningLists.scope: Specially Designated Nationals and Blocked Persons — U.S. persons prohibited from dealing
Entities requiring BIS license for export/reexport of items subject to EAR
Persons denied export privileges — no exports to these parties
Persons, groups, entities subject to EU financial sanctions and asset freezes
Global mandatory sanctions — all UN member states must implement
UK financial sanctions — mandatory for UK persons and UK-nexus transactions

_catalog.disclaimer: This catalog reflects sanctions programs as of the effective date. Sanctions change frequently — sometimes daily. Always verify against current official sanctions lists before processing any transaction involving flagged jurisdictions. This catalog is for risk-awareness and screening-trigger purposes only.

keyRestrictions: Most goods prohibited for export from US/EU/UK to Russia
Financial transactions with Russian entities severely restricted
SWIFT disconnection for major Russian banks
Oil price cap: crude ≤$60/barrel, petroleum products ≤$45-100/barrel (G7)
Aviation: no EU/US aircraft sales/leasing to Russia; EU/UK airspace closed
Steel, coal, seaborne oil import from Russia banned in EU
Maritime: insurance/reinsurance for Russia-destined ships restricted
allowedExceptions: Food and agricultural commodities (OFAC General Licenses 4, 6B)
Medicine and medical devices (OFAC General Licenses 4, 6B)
Energy: certain energy-related transactions authorized (General License 8C, transitional)
redFlags: Shipments routing through Belarus, Turkey, Kazakhstan, UAE, Armenia, Georgia — common circumvention routes
Shipping goods on Russia's shadow/dark fleet vessels
Any connection to listed Russian oligarchs or their family members
Goods with no commercial purpose for stated destination
Shell company intermediaries in non-sanctioned countries
jurisdictions.antiCircumventionNote: EU, UK, US, and G7 actively enforce anti-circumvention rules. Third-country companies facilitating Russia sanctions evasion face secondary sanctions and enforcement action.

keyRestrictions: COMPREHENSIVE: Iranian Transactions and Sanctions Regulations (ITSR) prohibit virtually all transactions
U.S. persons prohibited from engaging in most transactions with Iran
No import of Iranian goods into US
Secondary sanctions: non-US persons dealing significantly with Iran face U.S. sanctions
SWIFT: Iran effectively disconnected
UN Resolution 2231: arms embargo, nuclear-related restrictions
allowedExceptions: Food and medicine under TSRA General License
OFAC Specific License for other categories
redFlags: UAE addresses used by Iranian companies (known hub for Iran evasion)
IRGC-affiliated entities or individuals
Persian language documentation
Goods transiting UAE, Turkey, Iraq, or Oman without clear commercial purpose
End-user refusing to provide details
Products with obvious Iranian market applications (oil equipment, aerospace parts)

keyRestrictions: UN Security Council has imposed near-total trade embargo
U.S. NKSR: comprehensive sanctions on virtually all transactions
Virtually no authorized trade
Arms embargo: complete
Oil imports capped at 4 million barrels/year under UN resolutions
redFlags: Ship-to-ship transfers in international waters
Falsified ship positions (AIS spoofing)
Chinese intermediaries for goods with clear DPRK end-use
Any vessel known to have called at North Korean ports

keyRestrictions: U.S. persons prohibited from most transactions with Cuba
U.S. exports to Cuba severely limited
Helms-Burton (LIBERTAD) Act: allows lawsuits against entities trafficking in confiscated U.S. property
Non-U.S. entities: no U.S. secondary sanctions (unlike Iran/Russia) — but U.S.-nexus transactions prohibited
redFlags: Cuban military entities (GAESA controls large portion of Cuban economy)
Transactions routed to avoid US dollar clearing

keyRestrictions: U.S. Syrian Sanctions Regulations: comprehensive prohibition on dealing with Syria
EU and UK comprehensive sanctions on Syrian government entities
UN arms embargo
Oil trade prohibited
allowedExceptions: Some support to non-Assad controlled areas
redFlags: Lebanese intermediaries for Syria-destined goods
Turkish border crossing points used for Syria transit

keyRestrictions: EU: multiple sanctions packages targeting Lukashenko regime
Listed entities and individuals: full asset freeze
Russia-Belarus circumvention: goods destined for Russia cannot be re-routed via Belarus
Export controls on technology, aviation, oil to Belarus
Listed state-owned entities: Belaruskali, Beltelecom, etc.
redFlags: Goods with Russia connection routing via Belarus
Connections to listed Lukashenko regime members
Potash/fertilizer from Belaruskali (major sanctioned entity)

keyRestrictions: OFAC E.O. 13884: blocking transactions with Venezuelan government
PDVSA (state oil company) sanctioned
Gold trade with Venezuelan government prohibited (EO 13850)
Non-government entities generally not comprehensively sanctioned
allowedExceptions: Certain oil transactions authorized temporarily (2023 OFAC General License)
redFlags: PDVSA connections
Venezuelan government entities as buyers
Gold exports from Venezuela

keyRestrictions: Military entities sanctioned: Tatmadaw leaders, military-owned conglomerates (MEHL, MEC)
Jade and rubies: import to US prohibited (JADE Act)
No comprehensive sanctions on civilian entities
Strong ASEAN ties remain — trade continues with civilian sector
redFlags: Connections to Myanmar military entities (MEHL, MEC, Tatmadaw commanders)
Gemstones (jade, rubies) — JADE Act prohibition
Goods likely to be used for military purposes

sanctioningBodies: U.S. BIS (Entity List, Unverified List, Military End-User List)
keyRestrictions: No comprehensive OFAC sanctions on China — but extensive export controls
BIS Entity List: hundreds of Chinese entities requiring license for US exports
October 2022 China chip rules: advanced semiconductors and manufacturing equipment restricted
Military End-User List: Chinese military-linked companies — license required for EAR items
Foreign Direct Product Rule (FDPR): US technology/equipment rules even for non-US made goods with US content
Huawei, ZTE: on Entity List — no supply of EAR items without BIS license
HIKVISION, Dahua: on Entity List for human rights concerns
allowedExceptions: EAR99 goods (not on CCL) to non-listed Chinese entities: generally no license required
Non-controlled civil goods to civilian end-users not on lists
redFlags: Any Huawei or affiliate in supply chain
Advanced chip or semiconductor equipment destined for China
Chinese military academy or PLA-affiliated research institutes as end-users
SMIC or other CMDC-listed entities
Goods with AI/surveillance applications (HIKVISION, Dahua supply chains)

sanctioningBodies: N/A — Ukraine is sanction target recipient, not imposing party
keyRestrictions: CRIMEA: U.S. OFAC prohibits most trade with Crimea region
EU: Crimea/Sevastopol sanctions — no import/export/trade with Russian-controlled area
Russian-occupied territories (post-2022): treated as Russian territory for sanctions purposes
Non-occupied Ukraine: no sanctions — EU/US supportive of Ukrainian trade
allowedExceptions: Non-occupied Ukraine: trade generally permitted and encouraged
redFlags: Delivery address in Crimea, Donetsk, Luhansk regions
Goods with military application entering conflict zone

keyRestrictions: Hezbollah and affiliates comprehensively sanctioned (OFAC, EU, UN)
Lebanon state entities not comprehensively sanctioned
Lebanon financial system: Banque du Liban and many banks under scrutiny post-financial collapse
Gold: Hezbollah financing through gold trade scrutinized
redFlags: Hezbollah or affiliated entities
Transactions designed to circumvent Lebanon financial restrictions

keyRestrictions: No comprehensive sanctions
FATF: Pakistan on grey list periodically — enhanced AML scrutiny for financial transactions
Export controls: sensitive technology controls apply (dual-use, military)
Nuclear concerns: ISI and nuclear-related entities of concern
redFlags: Nuclear research institutions
ISI-affiliated entities
Military end-users for dual-use technology

keyRestrictions: No comprehensive Iraq sanctions (removed after Saddam era)
ISIS-affiliated entities remain listed
Dual-use goods: export controls apply
Iran nexus risk: Iraq used for Iran sanctions evasion
redFlags: ISIS-affiliated entities
Iran-nexus transactions using Iraq as transit
Military end-users for controlled technology

keyRestrictions: UAE itself is not sanctioned
MAJOR CIRCUMVENTION HUB: UAE is the #1 identified hub for Russia, Iran, and other sanctions evasion
G7 and EU have issued warnings and secondary sanctions threats to UAE entities
FATF grey list: UAE periodically listed — enhanced AML scrutiny
redFlags: UAE company with Russian beneficial owners
UAE forwarding goods likely destined for Russia or Iran
Shell companies with no physical presence
Dual-use goods with no clear UAE end-use
Russian-owned vessels calling UAE ports
jurisdictions.specialNote: UAE is NOT sanctioned but is the primary documented circumvention hub for US/EU/UK sanctions on Russia and Iran. Enhanced due diligence required for UAE-based intermediaries.

keyRestrictions: Turkey not sanctioned
Documented Russia circumvention hub — second largest after UAE
G7 issued specific warnings to Turkey on Russia sanctions compliance
Turkey maintains independent policy — trades with Russia despite NATO membership
redFlags: Turkish intermediaries routing goods to Russia
Dual-use goods with no clear Turkish end-use
Russian-connected Turkish entities

keyRestrictions: India not sanctioned and not a Russia circumvention hub per se
India maintains strategic autonomy — imports Russian oil, weapons
US/EU monitor re-export of controlled goods from India to Russia
Dual-use and export-controlled goods exported to India require end-use monitoring
redFlags: Dual-use goods to Russian-connected Indian companies
Technology goods with India end-use likely re-exported to Russia or China

fatfHighRiskJurisdictions.description: FATF High-Risk Jurisdictions subject to a Call for Action (formerly 'Black List'). Financial transactions with these jurisdictions require enhanced due diligence.
fatfHighRiskJurisdictions.note: FATF list updated February and June each year. Always verify current list.

fatfIncreasedMonitoring.description: FATF Jurisdictions Under Increased Monitoring (Grey List). Not high-risk but require enhanced vigilance.
fatfIncreasedMonitoring.note: FATF grey list updated February and June each year. Verify current list before processing financial transactions.
